title: egypt_civilization/reaserch_papers_1903.10458v1
paragraphs: The glaze forms on the outside of the object during firing, resulting in little to no inter-particle glass in the core. 1983) There has been much debate and speculation about the validity of this method. While it's impossible to determine the glazing method with certainty due to the complexities of hybrid techniques, microstructure provides valuable information that enables the sorting of objects into groups based on their microstructure.

Museum objects require sampling, a challenge that hinders the study of entire collections. The OCT operating at 930nm, which falls within the absorption band of Cu2+ ions responsible for the turquoise color of faience objects, exhibited limited probing depth. This resulted in the inability to image the underlying structure beyond the glaze and interaction layers in most cases. Limited probing depth prevented the OCT from imaging all layers of the Egyptian faience microstructure. 2 Long-wavelength OCT at 2 μm is based on a Michelson interferometer with a broadband laser source.

Brighter areas in an OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography) image represent regions of high reflectivity. Dark areas indicate minimal changes in refractive index, resulting in fewer photons being scattered back from these regions. The OCT images of Egyptian Faience exhibit differences in microstructure. The interpretation of these images is crucial for understanding how the microstructure varies between objects. The depth resolution has decreased. 5 micrometers (930 nanometers OCT) (Liang et al.) 2012. A study by Cheung (2015) found that at a depth of ~7 micrometers (~2 micrometers using OCT), small features or details close together, such as thin gel layers less than 7 micrometers in the glaze due to weathering, become unresolvable.

OCT allows intact objects to be imaged over a large enough area to be representative of the whole object. In contrast, SEM requires sampling and each polished section provides only one cross-sectional image.
